topic: Someone's childhood imaginary friend appears in their niece's drawings
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips. I slammed the sketchbook shut with a force that made the nearby water glass rattle precariously. My jaw was tight, the muscles in my neck corded with tension. "This is ridiculous," I muttered, shoving the book under the bed. I paced the cramped living room, the floorboards protesting with every heavy step. Then, I saw it. Tucked beneath a vibrant crayon depiction of a rainbow, was a sketch I didn't recognize. It was a small, spindly figure with oversized, luminous eyes, standing beside a lopsided house. It was exactly like Barnaby, the imaginary companion from my earliest days. A hot flush crept up my neck. I snatched the drawing, my breath catching in my throat. It was a mocking echo of a time when I felt utterly powerless, and now, it was manifesting in my niece’s innocent creations.

[story 2]

I stared at the drawing, my knuckles white where I gripped the pencil. The air in the room seemed to thicken, pressing down on my chest until it was hard to draw a full breath. Every nerve ending felt frayed, buzzing with a sharp, restless energy. My hands trembled slightly as I examined the lines—the exaggerated proportions, the familiar, slightly unsettling silhouette of the little boy I used to invent. A low, frustrated sound escaped my lips, a tight expulsion of air that vibrated in my throat. I felt a surge of sheer exasperation. This was meant to be harmless, a sweet moment captured in colored wax, yet here it was, an intrusion, a ghost from a forgotten landscape bleeding into my niece's world. I wanted to rip the paper in half, to erase the evidence of this unwelcome visitation.

[story 3]

I stood frozen, my gaze locked onto the page. A deep, simmering heat radiated from my core, making my skin feel prickly and too tight. I watched the imaginary friend in the drawing—a creature of shadow and exaggerated whimsy—and a wave of intense irritation washed over me. It wasn't just seeing a memory; it was witnessing something unwelcome taking root in the space of my niece's innocent imagination. I clenched my fists so hard my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. "Stop it," I whispered, the sound rough and strained. The desire to physically shatter the paper, to obliterate the image before it could cause any more disturbance, was overwhelming. I took a ragged breath, trying to force the frantic energy inside me to settle, but it only churned harder.
